economist.argument: The $45M allocation works out to $19.6M per kilometre — 12% below the San Francisco DPW average for complete streets projects. INRIX 2024 data shows SF loses $1.8B annually to traffic congestion. A successful Van Ness BRT corridor could reduce peak-hour transit times by 18 minutes and generate a 2.4x ROI over 10 years. But Bay Area Infrastructure's last comparable project on Geary ran $3.1M over budget before liquidated damages were triggered. My conditional recommendation: introduce a $45,000 per week penalty structure for delays beyond Month 9, with funds escrowed to the small business disruption program.

advocate.argument: I want everyone in this room to think about Jasmine Chen — a single mother whose Route 47 adds 35 minutes every day this road is excavated. Tony Ricci will watch 55 percent of his family restaurant revenue disappear for eight months because his Van Ness storefront entrance is blocked. Earl Washington, 72 and diabetic, cannot safely walk to his CPMC appointment when the Van Ness sidewalks are torn up. Amy Park's students at Tenderloin Community School will breathe elevated PM2.5 every school day during Block B construction. I am demanding a $500,000 displaced business compensation fund and mandatory air quality monitoring before Block B begins.

engineer.argument: Van Ness Avenue runs over a known zone of Bay mud and filled land — the most recent subsurface survey is four years old, pre-dating the 2022 Embarcadero utility findings. The underground utility corridor on Van Ness has not been comprehensively mapped since 2019. Excavation during winter atmospheric river season carries a 35 percent productivity reduction risk based on comparable SF corridor data. The 2022 SOMA utility corridor breach cost DPW three weeks and $2.8M in emergency remediation. I am specifying full GPR ground-penetrating radar scan of all three blocks before excavation — the cost is $180,000 and it is non-negotiable. The 12-month timeline is achievable only if weather windows are built into the schedule, not treated as contingency.

watchdog.argument: Bay Area Infrastructure Inc. is pre-flagged — they delivered the 2022 Geary Boulevard project 6 weeks late with no performance bond. DPW General Contractors LLC completed 3 of 3 projects on time but has a pending SF Ethics Commission inquiry into procurement practices. Golden Gate Civil Works has zero prior entries in the SF contractor performance database — sole-source awarding above $500K requires Board of Supervisors approval under SF Admin Code Section 21.3. The 2021 Civic Center plaza project saw $1.2M in ghost labor charges before a whistleblower from the construction crew intervened. My requirements are non-negotiable: escrow payment milestones, monthly third-party audit, and a public expenditure dashboard updated weekly on the SF DPW website.

climate.argument: This project will remove 47 mature street trees providing an estimated 310 tonnes of annual carbon sequestration along the 2.3 kilometre Van Ness stretch. PM2.5 levels near Tenderloin Community School will rise an estimated 38 percent above BAAQMD baseline during Block B construction based on the agency's own 2023 construction modelling. There is a 71 percent probability the project will lose 14 to 18 working days to atmospheric river events based on NOAA decadal data. A SPUR 2023 induced demand study shows 4-lane urban arterials in San Francisco generate 19 percent more vehicle traffic within 24 months of opening. My counter-proposal: remove the third vehicle travel lane from Blocks A and B and replace it with a protected cycle track — this delivers complete streets design, not just wider roads.

sentences.text: The $45M allocation works out to $19.6M per kilometre — 12% below the San Francisco DPW average for complete streets projects.
I want everyone in this room to think about Jasmine Chen — a single mother whose Route 47 adds 35 minutes every day this road is excavated.
Van Ness Avenue runs over a known zone of Bay mud and filled land — the most recent subsurface survey is four years old, pre-dating the 2022 Embarcadero utility findings.
Bay Area Infrastructure Inc. is pre-flagged — they delivered the 2022 Geary Boulevard project 6 weeks late with no performance bond.
This project will remove 47 mature street trees providing an estimated 310 tonnes of annual carbon sequestration along the 2.3 kilometre Van Ness stretch.
INRIX 2024 data shows San Francisco loses $1.8 billion annually to traffic congestion on its core arterial network.
Tony Ricci will watch 55 percent of his family restaurant revenue disappear for eight months because his Van Ness storefront entrance is blocked.
Excavation during winter atmospheric river season carries a 35 percent productivity reduction risk based on comparable San Francisco corridor data.
DPW General Contractors LLC has a pending SF Ethics Commission inquiry into procurement practices that has not been resolved.
PM2.5 levels near Tenderloin Community School will rise an estimated 38 percent above BAAQMD baseline during Block B construction.
A successful Van Ness BRT corridor could reduce peak-hour transit times by 18 minutes and generate a 2.4x return on investment over 10 years.
Earl Washington, 72 and diabetic, cannot safely walk to his CPMC appointment when the Van Ness sidewalks are torn up for eight months.
The 2022 SOMA utility corridor breach cost DPW three weeks and $2.8 million in emergency remediation — the same risk applies here without a GPR survey.
Golden Gate Civil Works has zero prior entries in the SF contractor performance database — sole-source awarding above $500K requires Board of Supervisors approval under SF Admin Code Section 21.3.
There is a 71 percent probability the project will lose 14 to 18 working days to atmospheric river events based on NOAA decadal weather data.
Bay Area Infrastructure's last comparable project on Geary ran $3.1 million over budget before liquidated damages were triggered.
Amy Park's students at Tenderloin Community School will breathe elevated PM2.5 every school day during Block B construction — three of them have diagnosed asthma.
I am specifying full GPR ground-penetrating radar scan of all three blocks before excavation begins — the $180,000 cost is non-negotiable.
The 2021 Civic Center plaza project saw $1.2 million in ghost labor charges before a whistleblower from the construction crew intervened.
A SPUR 2023 induced demand study shows 4-lane urban arterials in San Francisco generate 19 percent more vehicle traffic within 24 months of opening.
My conditional recommendation: introduce a $45,000 per week penalty structure for delays beyond Month 9, with funds escrowed to the small business disruption program.
I am demanding a $500,000 displaced business compensation fund and mandatory air quality monitoring before Block B construction begins.
The 12-month timeline is achievable only if atmospheric river windows are built into the schedule as planned gaps, not treated as contingency buffers.
My requirements are non-negotiable: escrow payment milestones, monthly third-party audit, and a public expenditure dashboard updated weekly on the SF DPW website.
My counter-proposal: remove the third vehicle travel lane from Blocks A and B and replace it with a protected cycle track — complete streets design, not just wider roads.
